usage: Inspiration metadata only. Do not copy, reproduce, publish, or redistribute third-party creatives.

references.layout: native paid-social creative with one clear visual hook, simple message, and direct CTA
references.promptNotes: Make the ad feel native to Meta placements: high contrast focal image, one headline, one benefit, one CTA, no clutter.

references.layout: designer-grade product ad with bold typography, rounded product frames, and clean shoppable hierarchy
references.promptNotes: Use polished designer composition: strong headline, product hero, benefit pills, tidy spacing, premium color palette.

references.layout: product stack with 3-5 small benefit pills and a clear shop CTA
references.promptNotes: Create a premium product stack with small benefit labels, not too many words, and a visual hierarchy that reads in one second.

references.layout: before/after split with a clear emotional transformation and minimal labels
references.promptNotes: Use a tasteful before/after structure without attacking competitors. Make the after outcome aspirational and credible.

references.layout: meme-inspired simple ad with native humor, one problem line, and clean branded footer
references.promptNotes: Make it feel social-native and funny without looking cheap. One relatable phrase, one visual joke, one product resolution.

references.layout: product scene with chat/testimonial overlay and star/social proof cue
references.promptNotes: Use a native testimonial bubble layered over a premium product photo. Keep copy short and believable.

references.layout: hero product with fine callout lines to 3 differentiating features
references.promptNotes: Use callout lines to explain why the product is better. Keep labels small and precise, like a premium product diagram.

references.layout: bold discount poster with product cutout, one large promo, and strong CTA
references.promptNotes: Make the offer impossible to miss while keeping the ad premium. Use one large discount or bundle line only.

references.layout: creator-style vertical frame with product in hand and a short caption overlay
references.promptNotes: Make it look like a high-quality creator ad, not a studio poster. Human proof, natural light, short caption.

references.layout: lifestyle skin moment with product bottle, ingredient/nature cue, and safety headline
references.promptNotes: For beauty or wellness, combine aspirational lifestyle, product hero, and one safety/ingredient claim.

references.layout: product centered with ingredient textures and concise mechanism copy
references.promptNotes: Use ingredients as visual proof. Make formula/mechanism feel premium and understandable.

references.layout: problem headline, product answer, clean proof subline, CTA button
references.promptNotes: Lead with a concrete skin/problem tension, then visually present the product as the simple solution.

references.layout: white or soft color canvas, large product, editorial typography, generous negative space
references.promptNotes: Use fewer elements, cleaner typography, and premium whitespace. The ad should feel expensive, not loud.

references.layout: editorial/news style card with headline, product photo, and small proof bullets
references.promptNotes: Use a magazine/editorial layout to borrow authority. Do not imply real press unless provided by user.

references.layout: back-in-stock announcement with product grid and CTA
references.promptNotes: Use scarcity/availability without pressure spam. Make the product feel demanded and easy to buy.

references.layout: two-column comparison table with friendly checkmarks and one recommended product
references.promptNotes: Compare old way vs new way, not us vs them. Make the difference obvious and fair.

references.layout: product-focused image ad with one benefit-led headline and visible CTA
references.promptNotes: Make the product the hero. Use clear conversion intent, not vague branding.

references.layout: service ad with clear promise, credibility element, and lead CTA
references.promptNotes: For service businesses, make the outcome and credibility obvious. Avoid product-photo assumptions.

references.layout: customer quote block with product/service context and CTA
references.promptNotes: Make the quote readable and specific. Use one believable proof statement, not generic praise.

references.layout: discount/bonus card with product visual and one CTA
references.promptNotes: Make offer math easy to understand: discount, bundle, free shipping, or bonus. One primary number only.

references.layout: in-market static ad with unmistakable product, clear reason to buy, and simple CTA
references.promptNotes: Prioritize the creative decisions that usually scale: visual hook, one message, one CTA, easy product comprehension.

references.layout: large question/pain headline, product/service as answer, short CTA
references.promptNotes: Use a painfully specific headline. Visual should instantly show the solution.

references.layout: benefit headline, product supporting visual, small feature proof
references.promptNotes: Lead with the customer benefit, then use one feature to make it credible.

references.layout: one scroll-stopping visual, one clear message, one CTA, one proof element
references.promptNotes: Use the four-part Meta ad discipline: hook visual, clear message, CTA, trust cue.

references.layout: vertical story creative with top hook, center visual, bottom CTA safe zone
references.promptNotes: Design for 9:16 safe zones. Strong hook at top, clean CTA at bottom, no important text near edges.

references.layout: hook headline, product visual, reason to believe, CTA
references.promptNotes: Use a formulaic static ad structure: hook, product, proof/reason, CTA. Avoid decorative filler.

references.layout: unexpected visual metaphor with product placed as resolution
references.promptNotes: Start with an image idea before copy. The visual must make someone stop before reading.

references.layout: simple Facebook ad with strong hook copy and easy visual context
references.promptNotes: Keep it very simple. If the ad cannot be understood in one second, reduce elements.

references.layout: clean visual comparison of pain/old way vs better/new way
references.promptNotes: Use comparison as education, not aggression. No competitor names unless user provided.

references.layout: ad built around one measurable conversion action: lead, sale, call, or trial
references.promptNotes: Make the objective visible in the CTA. The visual should support the action, not just look nice.

references.sourceName: Common Thread Collective Creative Facebook Ads
references.layout: hook, problem, product, proof, action
references.promptNotes: Use a five-part ad anatomy. Every element must earn its place: hook, problem, product, proof, action.

references.sourceName: Common Thread Collective Creative Facebook Ads
references.layout: testable creative type with a single hypothesis
references.promptNotes: Design the ad as one testable hypothesis, not a general brand poster.

references.layout: clean social card with platform-like proportions, product cutout, headline, CTA
references.promptNotes: Use clean ecommerce ad grammar: product hero, benefit headline, CTA button, optional rating/offer.

references.layout: bright colorful product scene with bold hook text and product surrounded by category props
references.promptNotes: Use color and props for attention, but keep the product and headline dominant.

references.layout: result metric headline with product/service visual and credible proof cue
references.promptNotes: Only use metrics if user provided them. Otherwise use qualitative proof like reviews, process, or guarantee.

references.layout: energetic collage of bundle elements with one bundle/save headline
references.promptNotes: Use collage only if it stays legible. Show bundle abundance and one savings reason.

references.layout: playful pastel or bold graphic offer poster with product and large discount
references.promptNotes: Use youthful visual energy, large offer text, and playful product treatment for fashion/food/beauty.

references.layout: editorial catalog layout with product cutout, style notes, and CTA
references.promptNotes: For apparel, make the item inspectable, styled, and clearly shoppable.

references.layout: product shot with small rounded benefit tags and soft premium background
references.promptNotes: Use compact benefit chips around product. Keep it premium and calm.

references.layout: category-agnostic concept adapted to product and ICP
references.promptNotes: Adapt the concept to the buyer psychology, not the original niche. Product changes, idea stays.

references.layout: one hypothesis per ad, simple variation across hook/visual/offer
references.promptNotes: Create distinct hypotheses, not visual duplicates. Each ad must test one new reason to click.
